pick.rationale: Arizona enters this Round of 32 matchup as one of the nation's elite programs — KenPom #3 and Torvik #3 with an AdjEM of +37.56, the 3rd-best defense in the country (AdjDE 89.7), and a top-10 offense (AdjOE 126.2) forged against elite Big 12 competition capped by a conference tournament championship over Houston. Utah State is a genuinely dangerous 9-seed at 28-6 with Mountain West regular season and tournament titles, an ORtg of 120.0 (20th nationally), and MWT MVP MJ Collins Jr. leading a high-octane attack, but their defense ranked 70th nationally (DRtg 102.3) is a meaningful liability against Arizona's elite offensive firepower. The efficiency gap between these teams is approximately 20 points, and Utah State's Mountain West schedule — while respectable — did not prepare them for the two-way excellence Arizona brings, particularly a defense that ranks among the three best in the country. No significant injury concerns were reported for either team, and the combination of Arizona's superior efficiency margin, elite defensive profile, and Big 12 battle-testing make the Wildcats a clear and confident pick to advance to the Sweet 16.

reasoningStep.title: Arizona vs. Utah State — Round of 32 West Region Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Arizona holds a commanding ~20-point efficiency advantage over Utah State, anchored by the 3rd-best defense in the country and a top-10 offense built against elite Big 12 competition. Utah State is a credible 9-seed with a 28-6 record and the nation's 20th-ranked offense, but their defense ranked 70th nationally cannot contain Arizona's firepower. No injury concerns tip the balance, and the efficiency gap combined with schedule-strength differential make Arizona a clear favorite.
evidence: Arizona: KenPom #3, Torvik #3, AdjEM +37.56, AdjOE 126.2 (7th nationally), AdjDE 89.7 (3rd nationally), 31-2 record, Big 12 Tournament champions over Houston.

Utah State: Sports-Reference SRS 17.36 (33rd nationally), ORtg 120.0 (20th nationally), DRtg 102.3 (70th nationally), 28-6 record, Mountain West regular season and tournament champions; MJ Collins Jr. named MWT MVP.
No significant injury concerns reported for either Arizona or Utah State heading into the Round of 32.
The ~20-point efficiency gap and 1-vs-9 historical win rate (~85%+) strongly favor Arizona; Utah State's Mountain West schedule did not prepare them for Arizona's elite two-way profile.

result.text: Depth concerns popping up for Arizona Wildcats men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues | Arizona Desert Swarm Skip to main content SB Nation SB Nation logo An Arizona Wildcats Community Arizona Desert Swarm March 17, 2026 Arizona Desert Swarm Notifications Notifications The Feed Sections Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Navigation Drawer Login / Sign Up close Close Search The Feed Sections Expand Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting Expand FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Community Guidelines Newsletters All communities Facebook Twitter Youtube RSS Arizona Desert Swarm Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues Notifications Notifications Arizona Basketball Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues by Brian Pedersen Feb 16, 2026, 10:00 AM UTC Facebook Link TUCSON, ARIZONA - FEBRUARY 14: Motiejus Krivas #13 and Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats walk off the court after losing to the Texas Tech Red Raiders 78-75 in overtime at McKale Center at ALKEME Arena on February 14, 2026 in Tucson, Arizona. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU. If Peat can’t go it will mark the first time this season the UA will send out a different starting five, but more importantly it means the 8-man rotation that has served

(Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU. If Peat can’t go it will mark the first time this season the UA will send out a different starting five, but more importantly it means the 8-man rotation that has served

modelTrace.content:
I now have sufficient data to make a well-informed pick. Let me compile everything:

**Arizona (1-seed):**
- KenPom #3, Torvik #3, AdjEM +37.56
- 31-2 record, Big 12 champions (beat Houston in title game)
- AdjOE 126.2 (7th nationally), AdjDE 89.7 (3rd nationally)
- Big 12 Tournament champions — elite two-way profile

**Utah State (9-seed):**
- Sports-Reference SRS: 17.36 (33rd nationally)
- 28-6 record, Mountain West regular season AND tournament champions
- ORtg: 120.0 (20th nationally), DRtg: 102.3 (70th nationally)
- MWT MVP: MJ Collins Jr. — All-MWC Second Team
- No significant injury concerns reported

**Matchup context:**
- Arizona's AdjEM of +37.56 vs. Utah State's implied ~+17 (based on SRS 33rd) = roughly 20-point efficiency gap
- Arizona's defense (3rd nationally, AdjDE 89.7) is a severe wall for Utah State's offense
- Utah State's defense (70th nationally, DRtg 102.3) is solid but not elite — Arizona's top-10 offense will find openings
- Utah State won the MWC sweep (regular season + tournament), showing genuine quality, but the Mountain West is a significant step below Big 12
- No injury concerns flagged for either team heading into this game
- The prior pick already committed Arizona winning R64 (vs. LIU) and Utah State winning R64 (vs. Villanova, with Villanova's injury context)
- 1-vs-9 matchups historically favor the 1-seed at ~85%+ rate; the efficiency gap here is substantial

Utah State is a genuinely dangerous 9-seed: 28-6 Mountain West regular season and tournament champions with an ORtg of 120.0 (20th nationally) and MWT MVP MJ Collins Jr. leading a high-octane offense, but their defense ranked 70th nationally (DRtg 102.3) is a meaningful liability against Arizona's elite offensive firepower. The efficiency gap between these teams is approximately 20 points, and Utah State's Mountain West schedule — while respectable — did not prepare them for the two-way excellence Arizona brings, particularly a defense that ranks among the three best in the country. No significant injury concerns were reported for either team, and the combination of Arizona's superior efficiency margin, elite defensive profile, and Big 12 battle-testing make the Wildcats a clear and confident pick to advance to the Sweet 16."
